caveats: a: Exact-industry staffing evidence is more than two decades old.
a: Broader NAICS 327 includes many processes unlike mineral wool.
a: The injected compensation ratio uses 2024 wages over 2022 receipts and is harmonized to the IPS basis as stated in the assignment.
rho: No source measures five-year implementation for the target firm population.
rho: Large producers' digital capabilities may not represent lower-middle-market operators.
e: Public company evidence demonstrates channel structure but not the ownership mix of the full LMM population.
e: The injected 45-firm count is a margin-bridged ESTIMATE rather than a directly observed EBITDA-band census.
s5: Small-business marketplace deals are not representative of target-size mineral-wool plants.
s5: Owner age does not measure sale intent or plant transferability.
s5: Asset sales and internal reorganizations must be excluded from qualifying transfers.
q: Owens Corning is much larger than the lens and has products outside NAICS 327993.
q: Public segment results do not identify AI-derived savings or contractual pass-through.
q: Volume and implementation effects are excluded from this primitive.
d5: Company data are global or multi-product and not a US industry census.
d5: Model-code efficiency gains do not guarantee state adoption or mineral-wool share.
d5: Foam, cellulose, wood fiber, and other insulation materials are substitutes.
o: Operator-required does not mean current staffing is durable; a highly automated plant still counts as the operator.
o: Certification and liability practices could change with new materials and modular systems.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Repeat product sales through distributors, lumberyards, home centers, installers, contractors, retailers, and direct industrial channels, with revenue driven by volume, product mix, service, location, quality, building specifications, and negotiated selling prices.
lens.excluded_activities: Insulation installers, distributors without manufacturing, captive internal plants without external recurring customers, metallic wool, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market firms that repeatedly manufacture mineral wool and fiberglass insulation from rock, slag, glass, or combinations thereof for external residential, commercial, industrial, acoustic, fire-protection, and equipment customers.

a.bridge: The exact-industry Census evidence is from 2002 and measures production-worker headcount rather than current wage-weighted tasks, while current BLS evidence covers broader NAICS 327. I use the historical concentration of 15,788 production workers among 19,318 employees, current broader-sector shares of 33.97% production and 28.00% transportation/material moving, and furnace-operator tasks to identify a predominantly physical continuous-process workforce. I assign exposure to office, order, planning, maintenance-triage, quality-documentation, customer-service, and analytical tasks. The old exact-industry data may overstate manual production if plants automated since 2002, but broad-sector data may understate this code's continuous-process specialization.
caveats: Exact-industry staffing evidence is more than two decades old.
Broader NAICS 327 includes many processes unlike mineral wool.
The injected compensation ratio uses 2024 wages over 2022 receipts and is harmonized to the IPS basis as stated in the assignment.
a.change_evidence: Current six-digit OEWS wage shares and task-time studies from fiberglass and stone-wool plants, explicitly separating existing control automation from remaining human work, would materially change the estimate.
a.rationale: Mineral wool production requires melting, fiberizing, binding, curing, cutting, packaging, maintenance, testing, and material movement. AI can assist process anomaly detection, scheduling, maintenance, inspection, order handling, and commercial work, but continuous plants already use automation and still need physical intervention and accountable quality control, limiting not-yet-automated exposure.

d5.bridge: Owens Corning and ROCKWOOL report company-level insulation volumes, outlook, and market drivers across products and geographies, while DOE reports energy-code savings and the energy significance of building envelopes; none directly forecasts US constant-price, constant-quality 327993 demand five years ahead. I anchor downside in 2025 insulation volume weakness and housing starts, and upside in US capacity investment, efficiency codes, renovation, fire-safe insulation, and the large existing-building stock. Company revenue outlook can overstate quantity because of price and mix, while broader insulation data can overstate mineral wool if foam or cellulose gains share.
caveats: Company data are global or multi-product and not a US industry census.
Model-code efficiency gains do not guarantee state adoption or mineral-wool share.
Foam, cellulose, wood fiber, and other insulation materials are substitutes.
d5.change_evidence: US shipment volumes by fiberglass and stone-wool product, inflation-adjusted channel sales, housing completions, retrofit square footage, code adoption, and announced capacity utilization through 2031 would materially change the range.
d5.rationale: Near-term residential demand is challenged: Owens Corning reported approximately 5% lower 2025 insulation volume and lower fourth-quarter housing starts. Longer-term need is supported by repair and remodeling, stable nonresidential demand, tightening efficiency requirements, fire and acoustic performance, and a vast retrofit base. DOE estimates the 2024 IECC improves residential site energy efficiency by 7.8%, and opaque envelopes affect 28% of building energy use.

caveats: Public company evidence demonstrates channel structure but not the ownership mix of the full LMM population.
The injected 45-firm count is a margin-bridged ESTIMATE rather than a directly observed EBITDA-band census.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level map of all 327993 establishments with ownership, external revenue share, product line, channel mix, plant economics, and normalized EBITDA would materially improve eligibility.
e.rationale: Independent mineral-wool manufacturers generally sell recurring products to external channels and therefore fit the repeat-outsourcing lens. The share is reduced for captive glass-fiber operations, vertically integrated business units, plants whose economics cannot be separated from a much larger group, and entities lacking transferable customer and operating relationships.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 327993 ($1,422,739,255) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 327993 ($7,428,929,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1465 (margins.json[32]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Paper/Forest Products EBITDA/Sales 14.65% (mid-range for wood/paper/plastics)); revenue/employee $543,766 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 327993); class <5: 33 firms, avg revenue/firm $782,788 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $114,678 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 27 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,579,148 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $231,345 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 27 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,777,481 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $553,401 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 32 firms, avg revenue/firm $10,462,781 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,532,797 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 13 firms, avg revenue/firm $28,881,923 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $4,231,202 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 16 firms, avg revenue/firm $409,262,125 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $59,956,901 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 45

caveats: Operator-required does not mean current staffing is durable; a highly automated plant still counts as the operator.
Certification and liability practices could change with new materials and modular systems.
o.change_evidence: Evidence on contractor self-manufacture, modular construction integrating insulation upstream, direct digital fulfillment without manufacturer accountability, and product substitution by application would materially change the estimate.
o.rationale: Demand that remains in year five requires physical melting or fiber processing, curing, cutting, testing, packaging, and delivery by an accountable manufacturer. Digital specification, ordering, and design tools can reduce transactions and labor but cannot supply a certified thermal, acoustic, or fire-protection material. Vertical integration and alternative insulation products affect quantity demand more than operator necessity within the surviving basket.

q.bridge: Owens Corning reports segment selling-price, volume, input-cost, and manufacturing-cost effects for a global insulation portfolio that includes foam and other products as well as fiberglass and stone wool, not discounted benefit retention for LMM 327993 firms. I use its evidence that lower manufacturing costs and higher selling prices can coexist to support initial retention, then haircut for explicit competition on price, service, location, and quality and for concentrated distribution channels. A scaled branded producer may retain more than an undifferentiated smaller supplier.
caveats: Owens Corning is much larger than the lens and has products outside NAICS 327993.
Public segment results do not identify AI-derived savings or contractual pass-through.
Volume and implementation effects are excluded from this primitive.
q.change_evidence: Realized price, rebate, gross-margin, and customer-renewal cohorts from independent mineral-wool producers before and after automation, split by distributor, retail, contractor, and industrial channels, would materially move the range.
q.rationale: Insulation is sold by unit through repeat channels rather than billed as labor hours, so an internal labor saving is not automatically passed through. Yet price competition, powerful distributors and home centers, capacity additions, substitute insulation materials, and periodic negotiations should transfer a large portion over five years. Brand, specification, proximity, fire performance, and service allow partial retention.

caveats: No source measures five-year implementation for the target firm population.
Large producers' digital capabilities may not represent lower-middle-market operators.
rho.change_evidence: Five-year before-and-after evidence from mineral-wool plants deploying AI for process control, vision inspection, predictive maintenance, scheduling, customer support, and administrative workflows would materially move the range.
rho.rationale: Office, planning, service, reporting, and bounded decision-support workflows can be implemented with current tools, while plant applications require integration with historians and controls, reliable sensor data, cybersecurity, validation, and human approval. Continuous furnaces, fire and worker safety, product certification, and costly downtime make operators cautious about autonomous process changes.

s5.bridge: BizBuySell's 2,303 sold manufacturing listings for 2021-2025 cover many industries and mostly much smaller firms, while Census owner-age tables are economy-wide. I translate the evidence into a lower five-year probability for eligible 327993 firms because continuous plants are capital-intensive, the industry includes large strategic owners, and environmental, energy, product-liability, channel, and capacity diligence narrows the buyer pool. Marketplace selection may overstate transfer liquidity, while confidential strategic transactions may understate it.
caveats: Small-business marketplace deals are not representative of target-size mineral-wool plants.
Owner age does not measure sale intent or plant transferability.
Asset sales and internal reorganizations must be excluded from qualifying transfers.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year transaction census for 327993 from PitchBook, GF Data, DealStats, SBA loans, environmental permit transfers, and strategic acquisition disclosures matched to the independent eligible-firm denominator would materially change the estimate.
s5.rationale: Manufacturing businesses do transfer, but an intact mineral-wool control transfer requires specialized production assets, trained teams, certifications, supply and energy arrangements, distribution access, and environmental compliance. The small injected target population makes annual availability thin.

principal_driver: Energy, fire, and acoustic requirements sustain repeat demand for certified physical insulation products.
principal_weakness: A capital-intensive, already-automated production process and thin transfer population limit the realizable opportunity.

sources.supports: Defines the industry as manufacturing mineral wool and fiberglass insulation products from rock, slag, glass, or combinations thereof.
sources.title: US Census Bureau NAICS definition: 327993 Mineral Wool Manufacturing

sources.supports: Reports 19,318 employees and 15,788 production workers for NAICS 327993 in 2002, used only as a historical exact-industry staffing proxy.
sources.title: US Census Bureau Economic Census: Mineral Wool Manufacturing, 2002

sources.supports: Describes the physical and monitoring occupation that operates or tends industrial heating equipment, including glass annealing and material processing.
sources.title: O*NET OnLine: Furnace, Kiln, Oven, Drier, and Kettle Operators

sources.supports: Documents insulation channels and demand drivers; reports 2025 insulation sales down 6%, volume down about 5%, higher selling prices of $27 million, lower manufacturing costs of $30 million, and competition on price, service, location, quality, and design.
sources.title: Owens Corning 2025 Annual Report on Form 10-K

sources.supports: Reports 2,303 sold manufacturing listings analyzed for 2021-2025, providing a broad proxy for manufacturing transfer liquidity.
sources.title: Manufacturing Business Valuation Multiples & Financial Benchmarks

sources.supports: Provides economy-wide employer-business owner characteristics including the Age of Owner table, used only as broad succession context.
sources.title: Annual Business Survey: Characteristics of Business Owners, 2022 Tables

sources.supports: Reports 1% local-currency revenue growth in 2025, a slightly positive 2026 outlook, and solid long-term US growth potential amid low construction activity in other regions.
sources.title: ROCKWOOL Annual Report Highlights 2025

sources.supports: Reports DOE's determination that the 2024 IECC improves residential efficiency, estimating 7.8% site-energy, 6.8% source-energy, and 6.6% energy-cost savings.
sources.title: US Department of Energy: Model Energy Code Determinations

sources.supports: States that opaque envelopes affect 28% of building energy use and that nearly 93% of residential and 60% of commercial buildings existing today will still exist in 2050, supporting retrofit relevance.
sources.title: US Department of Energy: Opaque Envelope

title: Mineral Wool Manufacturing
